exercises.title: Build Your Eisenhower Matrix
exercises.description: Create an Eisenhower Matrix for your current work week. List every task you need to accomplish and place each one in the correct quadrant. Then identify which Quadrant 3 tasks you can delegate and which Quadrant 4 tasks you should eliminate entirely.
hints: Be honest about what's truly urgent vs. what just feels urgent
Ask: 'Will this matter in 30 days?' to distinguish important from not-important
Most developers have too many items in Quadrant 1 — look for ways to move tasks to Quadrant 2

solution.code:
EISENHOWER MATRIX EXAMPLE:

DO FIRST (Urgent + Important):
• Fix production login bug (deadline: today)
• Complete code review for tomorrow's deploy
• Respond to client escalation email

SCHEDULE (Not Urgent + Important):
• Refactor authentication module (scheduled for Thursday)
• Write documentation for new API
• Learn TypeScript advanced patterns
• Exercise 3x this week

DELEGATE (Urgent + Not Important):
• Status report to management (use async update tool)
• Onboarding help for new dev (pair with senior dev)
• Meeting that could be an email (send async update)

ELIMINATE (Not Urgent + Not Important):
• Optimizing a page nobody visits
• Attending optional all-hands meetings
• Reading every Slack thread in #random

exercises.title: Time Audit Challenge
exercises.description: For 3 consecutive work days, log what you do in 30-minute blocks. At the end, categorize each block into Eisenhower quadrants. Calculate the percentage of time in each quadrant. Your goal: spend at least 40% of your time in Quadrant 2 within two weeks.
hints: Use a spreadsheet, Notion table, or simple notebook
Be honest — if you spent 30 minutes on Twitter, log it as Quadrant 4
Don't try to change your behavior during the audit — just observe

solution.code:
TIME AUDIT TEMPLATE:

| Time    | Task                    | Quadrant | Notes              |
|---------|-------------------------|----------|--------------------|
| 9:00 AM | Code review             | Q1       | Blocking deploy    |
| 9:30 AM | Slack messages          | Q4       | Wasted time        |
| 10:00 AM| Deep coding             | Q2       | API refactor       |
| 10:30 AM| Deep coding             | Q2       | API refactor       |
| 11:00 AM| Meeting (could email)   | Q3       | Should delegate    |
| 11:30 AM| Meeting                 | Q3       | Status update      |
| 12:00 PM| Lunch                   | -        |                    |
| 12:30 PM| Email/Slack             | Q4       | Low value          |
| 1:00 PM | Bug fix                 | Q1       | Customer reported  |
| 1:30 PM | Documentation           | Q2       | Learning benefit   |

QUARTERLY SUMMARY:
Q1: 25% | Q2: 35% | Q3: 25% | Q4: 15%

ACTION: Reduce Q3 by batching meetings, reduce Q4 by disabling non-essential notifications.

exercises.title: Design Your Ideal Week Template
exercises.description: Create a weekly calendar template that protects your peak energy hours for deep work. Include time blocks for: deep coding, meetings, email/communication, learning, exercise, and buffer time for unexpected issues.
hints: Identify your peak energy hours (for most devs: 9-11 AM)
Batch all meetings into 1-2 afternoon slots
Leave 30-minute buffers between blocks for transitions
Include exercise — it's not optional, it's productivity infrastructure

solution.code:
IDEAL WEEK TEMPLATE:

MONDAY-THURSDAY:
7:30 AM  - Morning routine + commute
9:00 AM  - Deep work block 1 (highest energy task)
11:00 AM - Code reviews / pair programming
12:00 PM - Lunch + walk
1:00 PM  - Meetings / collaboration (batched)
3:00 PM  - Deep work block 2 (moderate energy task)
4:30 PM  - Email, Slack, admin
5:00 PM  - End of day review + plan tomorrow

FRIDAY:
9:00 AM  - Weekly review + planning
10:00 AM - Learning / experimentation
12:00 PM - Lunch
1:00 PM  - Low-stakes work / documentation
3:00 PM  - Week wrap-up + celebration

KEY RULES:
1. No meetings before 1 PM on coding days
2. Deep work blocks = phone on silent, Slack closed
3. 30 min buffer between every block
4. Friday afternoon = no meetings, ever
